questions.question: You run `cmd > out.txt`. The command prints both normal output and errors. What ends up in `out.txt`?
options: Only the normal output; errors print to the terminal
Only the errors; normal output prints to the terminal
questions.explanation: `>` redirects fd 1 (stdout) only. Errors flow on fd 2 (stderr), which is still attached to the terminal. To capture both, use `cmd > out.txt 2>&1` or `cmd &> out.txt`.

questions.question: What does `Ctrl-Z` do to the foreground job?
options: Suspends it (SIGTSTP) so it can be resumed with `fg`/`bg`
questions.explanation: Ctrl-Z sends SIGTSTP, which stops the foreground process group. The shell now tracks it as a suspended job; resume with `fg %N` (foreground) or `bg %N` (background).

questions.question: A pipeline `false | true` exits with what status by default in bash, and how do you change it?
options: Exits 0 (the last command's exit code); use `set -o pipefail` to make it return the rightmost non-zero
Exits 0 always; pipeline status is undefined
questions.explanation: Without `pipefail`, a pipeline's exit code is the last command's exit code, so `false | true` returns 0 and hides the failure. `set -o pipefail` makes the pipeline return the rightmost non-zero exit — essential for CI scripts.

questions.question: You run `tail -f log | grep ERROR | tee out.txt` and nothing appears in `out.txt` even though errors are being logged. What's the most likely cause?
options: grep is block-buffering its stdout because it's piped, so tee never sees data until grep's 4-8 KiB buffer fills
questions.explanation: When stdout is a pipe (not a terminal), libc switches stdio to block-buffered mode by default. grep accumulates output until its internal buffer fills. Fix: `grep --line-buffered ERROR` or `stdbuf -oL grep ERROR`.

questions.question: What's the difference between `kill 12345` and `kill %1`?
options: `kill 12345` targets the OS PID 12345; `kill %1` targets the shell's job 1, which may be a multi-process pipeline
questions.explanation: `%N` is the shell's job notation — it resolves to the whole process group of job N, so it kills every process in the pipeline. PID-based kill targets only one process. In practice, prefer `%N` inside an interactive shell because it can't get the wrong PID after history scrollback.

questions.question: Why does `2>&1 > file` redirect differently than `> file 2>&1`?
options: It doesn't; bash treats them the same
Redirects apply left to right. `2>&1` first dups stderr to whatever fd 1 currently points to (the terminal). Then `> file` redirects fd 1 to file — but fd 2 still points to the terminal
The first form is invalid syntax
The first form uses a different shell
questions.explanation: Redirects are evaluated left to right and `2>&1` snapshots fd 1's current destination at that moment. To send both to a file, you must redirect fd 1 first, then dup fd 2 to it: `> file 2>&1`.
